advantages: Averages performance over k partitions, reducing noise from any single random train/validation split.
Uses nearly all labeled data for both training and validation across folds—efficient when n is small.
Stratified k-fold keeps class balance in each fold, making classification metrics more reliable on imbalanced data.
Fold score spread provides a practical stability diagnostic related to variance in generalization.
disadvantages: Costs k (or k×repeats) full model refits—prohibitive for large deep models or very large tabular grids.
Assumes samples are exchangeable; temporal, group, or user structure requires group-aware CV, not plain shuffled k-fold.
Repeated tuning on the same CV splits without nested structure can still yield optimistic scores.
Mean CV score is not a substitute for a locked test set when many experiments have already informed model choice.
